bibliographic_data.title: Garbage Collection for Multicore NUMA Machines
bibliographic_data.abstract: Modern high-end machines feature multiple processor packages, each of which contains multiple independent cores and integrated memory controllers connected directly to dedicated physical RAM. These packages are connected via a shared bus, creating a system with a heterogeneous memory hierarchy. Since this shared bus has less bandwidth than the sum of the links to memory, aggregate memory bandwidth is higher when parallel threads all access memory local to their processor package than when they access memory attached to a remote package. This bandwidth limitation has traditionally limited the scalability of modern functional language implementations, which seldom scale well past 8 cores, even on small benchmarks. This work presents a garbage collector integrated with our strict, parallel functional language implementation, Manticore, and shows that it scales effectively on both a 48-core AMD Opteron machine and a 32-core Intel Xeon machine.

core_content.novelty_claim: The work demonstrates a modern functional language that effectively utilizes a large number of modern NUMA multicore processors and presents a garbage collector that provides excellent performance on multicore, NUMA machines.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors describe the design and implementation of Manticore, a parallel functional language with a strict, mutation-free programming model. They integrate a novel garbage collector into this system to minimize required synchronization and maximize locality, which is crucial for scalability on larger machines.
outcomes: Demonstrated good utilization of all available cores on both 32 and 48 core machines.
Presented a garbage collector that avoids bottlenecks due to excessive memory traffic by combining with other aspects of the runtime architecture.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper addresses the challenges of implementing scalable functional parallel programming languages in modern multicore processors, particularly those with non-uniform memory access (NUMA) architectures. The authors present Manticore, a strict, mutation-free functional language designed to run on such systems. They integrate a novel garbage collector into this system that minimizes required synchronization and maximizes locality, leading to effective scaling on both 32-core Intel Xeon and 48-core AMD Opteron machines. The paper demonstrates the effectiveness of their approach through a set of small but representative benchmarks across various physical memory allocation strategies.
